item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the idea behind this (particularly the fact that the original host hates creating the clones) but the science behind it is a bit too shaky for me to upvote. Possibly a case of too much science, not enough anomaly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is going to sound very fussy, but the use of UN2814 is not appropriate for talking about laboratory quarantine and security. UN numbers are for dangerous goods and are specifically about packaging and documentation for transport. They don't have anything to do with how something is stored or handled in a facility. For that you would refer to biosecurity levels/ containment levels. BSL4 is the highest , although could be cute and refer to BSL5 which would imply foundation has extra levels of special safety precautions which seems entirely realistic.

I like the idea of using of real world technical references but in this case 'quarantine as per UN2814" sticks out as not making sense.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
this is so… so incredibly sad
+1 for making me Feel

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I upvoted it, but it has some ortographical mistakes that would be easily corrected. I'm just not doing so because I don't know if it's intentional or something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this one a lot. The scp-1242 is like a queen forced to reproduce for her hive. Concepts like this have been covered but this has a twist of its own.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Got a suggestion as to which one?

And honestly, while I like the idea of attaching him to a GOI, I think it'd play better to have that happen in a tale.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: could use an attachment to a group or something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I actually don't dislike this— which, for a depressed kid who makes copies of himself that all want to protect him, is notable. My biggest problem isn't so much the evolution-esque replication business as—

SCP-1242 has displayed bouts of chronic depression associated with post-traumatic stress disorder and long-term isolation. Subject avoids discussion of the onset of its condition, and refuses to give its name.

It's kinda woobie.

Not that he doesn't have reason to be, but you're already playing with some pretty common tropes. It would have been nice to see some psychological hook that made it a bit more compelling. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Self-replicating kid? I dunno.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Never before has this game quote been so fitting…

"This guy are sick."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Got any suggestions? The original idea came with a name, but once it fell through I was left scratching my head. The best I can come up with is something about a biomemetic boy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is really good. Aside from being well-written, it's got good follow through, which is to say you took the idea and ran with it to its conclusion. It's nice to see a good humanoid for once.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Needs a name on the main list.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love the extensive detail, though it might seem foreboding to some. I'm going to refrain from voting because my opinions are mixed at this point. The detail is nice, but it's still another creepy little kid SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah, I misunderstood your question. And yeah, if the transformation was faster, this would've made sense, but when it takes the course of several hours the Foundation should've been able to put down the wild ones before the transformations progressed too far.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was referring to this line here:

While initial casualties were low, casualties from infection of personnel by SCP-1242-1 were high, and nearly resulted in the loss of the site as secondary instances of SCP-1242-2 formed and began using perimeter teams' weapons to attempt incursion into the site (emphasis mine) before offsite response teams could achieve the level of containment necessary to sanitize the combat areas.

I get that there were uncontained SCP-1242-1 out in the wild, but then they infected security personnel during the initial attack. What I'm wondering is how the infected security personnel were able to start joining in the attack when they should have been quarantined.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
is the Foundation too stupid to quarantine people exposed to a known anomalous contagion?

I believe these were copies that were out in the wild before we grabbed the source.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Instances of SCP-1242-2 have been witnessed asking, "Are you my mummy?"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Have to downvote this. I feel like I've seen this or permutations of it a bunch of times before, and the addendum makes absolutely no sense to me (how are infected parties continuing to fight? is the Foundation too stupid to quarantine people exposed to a known anomalous contagion?).

However, despite the concept not grabbing me, it's written well, and I'd encourage the author to keep trying.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A couple of things, some minor, some less so:

First, I would replace the racial markers thing with "ethnic" or "ethnicity". Definitions of "race" vary wildly from culture to culture (Italians, Irish, and British all used to be considered separate races, for instance, and are all now just considered "white" in the USA), but ethnicity is genetically based.

Second, I'm somewhat confused about the biological basis for the mental effects. I get that the retrovirus restructures the victims' genetics and physical structure to match that our the source, but does that mean that it restructures the brain to match him as well? Because, even with an increased dopamine/serotonin, that would mean that they'd start thinking like him. I think that the horror might be increased if the brain wasn't reorganized (or at least, not fully), so the victims still thought of themselves as who they were before. You could still keep the militia thing, but justify it by saying that one (or more) of the previous victims had been military-trained.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Isn't there something involving mosquitoes that does something similar to this?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The premise. I mean, it's not weird enough for me to revel in the wtf, but it's too weird for me to feel like I get it, and ultimately I'm just unsatisfied and confused.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree with this statement.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hmm, this intrests me! Self-replicating boy, whose replicas are capable, and willing, to form a rudimentary milita to save him? Neato. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
mmm, it's good, i like it, but…

self termination

that one is bugging me. Suicide sounds better, seriously, unless the foundation decided to upgrade him from SCP to level 2 or more agent/researcher.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If he could control it, it would be a "power". As it is, it sucks pretty hard to be him.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Conditional upvote. This needs to be cleaned up a little and clarified but the idea of some poor schmuck involuntarily transforming everyone around him into loyal clones of himself. It's almost a power in that, yeah, he could build an army, but that's the opposite of what he wants and it brings him nothing but misery. I am down with that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This article would be a lot better if some (not necessarily all) of Khym's questions were answered.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Large block of text:

+ show block
– hide block

SCP-1242-1 contains the genetic makeup of SCP-1242,

So if analyzed by normal means, the virus appears to contain the entire genome of the boy? That would be an awful lot to squeeze into a virus. Of course, it being anomalous, the virus could be bigger on the inside on the outside (or something like that), but the Foundation would make a note of something like that. It would be equally anomalous if the virus had the expect amount of genetic material, yet still somehow managed to change the genome of the infected into the genome of the boy.

and acts to alter the physical appearance of infected individuals to match that of SCP-1242.

As far as I can tell, it goes much further than just altering the appearance of the body, and actually alters the entire physiology of the infected (right down to the genome).

SCP-1242-1a that are smaller in mass than SCP-1242 begin bloating and swelling.

It has been determined that nutritional supplementation substantially improves chances of survival at this stage for smaller instances,

1) When no nutritional supplements are provided, does the extra mass come from nowhere, or does the extra mass all come from the food they're being fed?

2) If the infected is the same size as the boy, do they need to eat more calories to sustain the transformation process? Or does the transformation process not require any biochemical energy?

Instances of SCP-1242-2 possess intelligence commensurate to that of SCP-1242,

1) Do they gain any of the skills or memories that the boy has?

2) Do they maintain any memory or skills from before the transformation?

SCP-1242-2 appear to have a drive to protect SCP-1242, and in the pursuit of that goal will seek out and infect individuals to propagate more instances of SCP-1242-2.

Do they somehow gain an awareness of the existence of the boy after the transformation, even if they've never seen him or been told about him?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just can't get behind another SCP that's only interesting because it self-replicates, and that's all I'm getting from this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it, although the containment procedures seem to indicate the SCP is worthy of Keter designation.

I would suggest editing the third paragraph to not mention the ultimate result of the virus (metamorphosis into a doppelganger), as realizing the end result mid-way through the infection log may be more horrifying.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Re: tags. Heard and understood. My apologies on that. Also, updated in an attempt to repair continuity errors. I hope it helps.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If I may ask, do you dislike it for the premise or is there something in the execution that needs repair?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
On the one hand, this is decently well written.

On the other hand, it's a little kid that swears in four languages, doesn't age, and apparently carries a virus that… makes copies of himself?

I'm on the fence here, and am withholding a vote until I can figure out how I feel about this.

Edit: Please don't touch the tags unless you know what you're doing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The fuck am I reading. Really.

item.title: Thanks for the feedback!
item.content:encoded:
Glad you like it! I was prepared for plenty of yelling for a first try.

The most honest answer to most of your quibbles is that I have the horrible habit of revising on the fly and forgetting to check my continuity. The bit with multiple languages is a holdover from previous versions that didn't hold up. The rest are my brain jumping back and forth between scientist jargon and English major. I'll give ya' three guesses which one's more "me" and the forst two don't count!

As to inconsistencies in symptoms/effect for varied ages and weights, I just plain forgot.

I will attempt repairs post-haste!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Agreed on all these counts and upvoted.
A couple more things:

Due to subject's proven capacity for self-harm, all requests made by subject must be approved by Site Director.

That sounds like any request WILL be approved. I'd recommend changing it so no one else gets that impression.

Subject is intelligent, and generally cooperative to Foundation personnel.

That should probably be "cooperative with", but I may be wrong.

But overall I like it!
Also, I removed a useless "]" at the end of your addendum and edited the title for you to comply with site standards.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is actually pretty good.

+ show block
– hide block

I like the use of real-world UN biological containment categorization, and wish that kind of thing was more common. I'd clean up the language a little bit, specifically the first paragraph of the description. The use of 'appears' to describe something just hits me the wrong way, and real scientists would never use 'kilos' to describe a weight. Kilo could just as easily mean kilowatt, kilometer, kiloday, or pretty much anything in the Metric system, and a couple other little things.

What happens if you infect a preteen girl, or anyone else that's smaller than the transformed subject. Surely they wouldn't get massive weight loss tied to voiding. The 'moderate doses of MDMA' sounds bad, because that's not scientific. Describe symptoms, rather than explaining what it is 'like'. The use of numbers like One (1) is awful and nobody should ever do it. Matching DNA with 98% efficacy wouldn't make you look exactly like somebody: me and my brother share 99.9% of our DNA, and we look nothing alike. 98% genetic similarity to a human is a chimpanzee. Why can this thing 'curse in five languages and speaks a little spanish'?

But those are all minor quibbles, good job, upvote.
